narrative:
Mike Crapo has represented Idaho in the United States Senate since 1999, a tenure so long it predates the invention of the smartphone, which is fitting for a man whose policy positions often seem stuck in a pre-digital age of accountability. A Harvard Law graduate and former Idaho state senator, Crapo ascended to the chairmanship of the powerful Senate Finance Committee in January 2025, giving him direct jurisdiction over more than half the federal budget, including tax policy, Social Security, and Medicare. He carries a lifetime conservative rating above 90 percent from the American Conservative Union. He also carries a 2012 DUI conviction from Virginia, a distinction that at least proves he is capable of making poor decisions outside the realm of fiscal policy [1][2].

On the twenty provisions of the 80% Bill, Crapo is a near-perfect mirror of opposition. He has expressed skepticism toward banning congressional stock trading, stating "I don't think that's a good idea" and pointing to the existing STOCK Act, a law so toothless that members of Congress routinely violate it with little consequence [3]. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, calling the program "bureaucratic price fixing under the guise of negotiation" and pushing instead his own Lower Costs, More Cures Act, a market-based alternative that pharmaceutical lobbyists found considerably more palatable [4]. As chairman of the Finance Committee, he has championed the extension and permanence of the 2017 Trump tax cuts through the so-called One Big Beautiful Bill, legislation that independent analysts found disproportionately benefits high-income earners through provisions like preserving the 37 percent top rate and the 20 percent pass-through deduction [5][6]. He has shown no interest in closing the carried interest loophole, taxing the ultra-wealthy, or funding Social Security through progressive revenue measures, despite acknowledging the program's solvency challenges. He joined Senate Republicans in blocking the DISCLOSE Act, which would have required dark money groups to reveal donors contributing more than $10,000 [7].

Follow the money, and the voting record starts to make perfect sense. Securities and investment firms, insurance companies, and commercial banks top the list of Crapo's career donors. In the 2019-2024 cycle alone, the securities and investment industry contributed over $706,000. His top individual contributor is UnitedHealth Group, the nation's largest health insurer, which has given nearly $120,000 over his career. Executives from Carlyle Group, Blackstone, Stephens Inc., and JPMorgan Chase fill out his donor rolls. In his 2016 reelection, Crapo received nearly as much money from Arkansas as from Idaho, because nineteen executives at a single Little Rock investment bank, Stephens Inc., all wrote checks. Just over half of his campaign funding comes from PACs, and 84 percent of his individual donations arrive from out of state [8][9]. For a senator who chairs the committees that regulate his donors' industries, the arrangement is less a conflict of interest than a business model.

Crapo is not a villain in any melodramatic sense. He is something more ordinary and more durable: a deeply institutionalized senator who has spent a quarter century ensuring that the financial industry's legislative preferences are treated as serious policy while popular reforms supported by supermajorities of Americans are dismissed as radical. The 80% Bill asks whether Congress will side with the people or the donors. Crapo's career answers that question with a twenty-six-year voting record and a donor list that reads like a Wall Street directory [10].

Susan Collins is the senior senator from Maine, a Republican who has built a nearly three-decade career on the brand of "moderate" -- a word she wields like a shield whenever her voting record drifts rightward. First elected in 1996, Collins sits on the powerful Appropriations Committee and is the ranking member on the Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions Committee, positions that place her at the crossroads of virtually every policy Americans overwhelmingly support [1]. She is, in theory, the exact senator who could break partisan gridlock on the 80% bills. In practice, she is often the reason the gridlock holds.

Consider the congressional stock trading ban, which polls show 95 percent of Mainers support [2]. Collins opposes it, arguing that existing insider trading laws and financial disclosure requirements are sufficient safeguards. This position is especially rich given that her husband, Tom Daffron -- a former Senate staffer who ran a lobbying firm that collected nearly $60 million in government contracts -- owns all the couple's stock holdings, a portfolio that grew roughly 77.5 percent in 2024, placing Collins eighth among all members of Congress for investment returns [3]. On the DISCLOSE Act, which would drag dark money into the sunlight, Collins has been the deciding vote against it -- killing the bill in 2010 by a single vote, then blocking it again in 2022 -- all while publicly lamenting the very dark money she votes to protect [4]. She voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug negotiation provisions, leaving pharmaceutical companies free to keep charging Americans top dollar [5].

Collins did author the Social Security Fairness Act, a genuinely bipartisan achievement that repealed provisions penalizing public servants like firefighters and police officers [6]. And she co-authored lobbying reform legislation early in her career that strengthened disclosure requirements and banned lobbyist-funded gifts. These are real accomplishments, but they are the exceptions that prove the rule: when a reform is narrow enough to avoid threatening her donor base, Collins will sign on. When it threatens the industries that fund her campaigns, she discovers procedural objections.

And those industries are generous. Over her career, Collins has received more than $1.17 million from the insurance sector and over $440,000 from pharmaceutical companies [7]. Private equity and investment firms contributed over $715,000 during the 2020 cycle alone -- more than any other congressional candidate that year -- with Blackstone chair Steve Schwarzman personally dropping $2 million into a Super PAC backing her reelection [8]. Meanwhile, only 2.3 percent of her itemized contributions over $200 came from actual Maine residents [1]. She represents Maine; Wall Street represents her.

The bottom line on Susan Collins is that she has perfected the art of sounding reasonable while voting reliably for the interests that write the checks. She will co-sponsor a lobbying reform bill one session and block dark money disclosure the next. She will express concern about drug prices while pocketing pharmaceutical money and voting against the bill that would lower them. In a Senate that desperately needs genuine moderates, Collins plays one on television -- and the performance has been worth millions.

Kat Cammack has represented Florida's 3rd District since 2021, winning the seat vacated by her former boss, Ted Yoho, for whom she served as deputy chief of staff for nearly a decade. Her biography is one of the more compelling in Congress: raised on a cattle ranch outside Denver, she and her family lost the property in 2011 and experienced homelessness, an ordeal she credits as the catalyst for her political career [1]. At 32, she became the youngest Republican woman ever elected to Congress. She now sits on the Energy and Commerce Committee and the Agriculture Committee, co-founded the Republican Women's Caucus alongside Senator Katie Britt, and authored the REINS Act, one of the most ambitious deregulatory proposals in recent memory [2]. She holds a Master's degree in national defense and strategic studies from the Naval War College, which lends a certain gravitas to her national security positions.

On the 80% Bill, Cammack has a handful of clear wins. She signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge, declaring that "a limit on the time an individual can serve brings new ideas to Capitol Hill" and "wears away at the culture of political corruption" [3]. She has been a forceful advocate for releasing the Epstein files, voting for full transparency and meeting personally with survivors of Epstein and Maxwell's crimes — getting visibly emotional during floor debate on the issue [4]. She co-sponsored the Prohibition of Agricultural Land for the People's Republic of China Act, aligning with the 80% Bill's goal of protecting U.S. farmland from foreign adversaries [5]. These are substantive positions taken with apparent conviction, not just press-release bravery.

The rest of the ledger is considerably thinner. Cammack has not co-sponsored or publicly supported legislation to ban congressional stock trading, end corporate PAC contributions, impose a lifetime lobbying ban, audit the Pentagon, negotiate Medicare drug prices, ban dark money, pass the DISCLOSE Act, enact paid family leave, mandate police body cameras, ban corporate purchases of single-family homes, close the carried interest loophole, or ban junk fees. On taxing the ultra-wealthy, she signed the Taxpayer Protection Pledge from Americans for Tax Reform, effectively promising never to raise taxes under any circumstances [6]. She voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug negotiation provisions, consistent with her party line. On the AUMF repeals and ending forever wars, she has offered no public position. The right-to-repair movement, which has natural allies on the Energy and Commerce Committee, has not earned her sponsorship either.

The fundraising picture offers context for these silences. In the 2024 cycle, 37% of Cammack's campaign funds came from PAC contributions, with large individual contributions making up another 35% [7]. Her committee assignments on Energy and Commerce and Agriculture place her in the direct path of lobbying from health insurers, pharmaceutical companies, agribusiness, and energy firms — industries with a vested interest in preventing exactly the kind of structural reforms the 80% Bill proposes. She has not disclosed stock trades of note, but neither has she championed the ban [8]. For someone who built a brand on fighting Washington corruption, the absence of any action on dark money, corporate PACs, or lobbying reform is conspicuous.

Kat Cammack's personal story is genuinely moving, and her work on term limits, the Epstein files, and farmland protection shows she is capable of breaking from the comfortable inertia of her caucus. But the gap between her anti-establishment rhetoric and her legislative record on campaign finance, corporate influence, and economic fairness suggests that the "culture of political corruption" she once pledged to dismantle remains, for now, a remarkably comfortable place to fundraise from.

Shelley Moore Capito is West Virginia's senior senator and a walking monument to the state's most enduring tradition: political dynasties built on coal dust and corporate cash. The daughter of former Governor Arch A. Moore Jr. — who served three terms as governor and six terms in Congress before pleading guilty to five felonies including mail fraud, tax fraud, and extortion in 1990 — Capito has spent over two decades in Washington proving that the apple doesn't fall far from the tree, it just hires better lawyers.

As the ranking member (and now chair) of the Senate Environment and Public Works Committee, Capito has positioned herself as coal country's chief defender in Congress, fighting EPA regulations and rolling back the Clean Power Plan while her campaign coffers overflow with contributions from the oil, gas, and mining industries that have defined her career. Her net worth, estimated between $2.6 million and $7.8 million, is padded by an active stock trading portfolio heavy on tech giants like Microsoft, Apple, and Meta — trades she continues to make while opposing legislation to ban congressional stock trading. The irony of a senator trading individual stocks while blocking transparency reforms is apparently lost on no one except Shelley Moore Capito.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Capito's record is a masterclass in opposition to popular reform. She voted against the For the People Act, calling it a "partisan effort to federalize elections." She opposed the Freedom to Vote Act's provisions for campaign finance disclosure and early voting access. She has taken no meaningful action on banning dark money, closing tax loopholes for the ultra-wealthy, or imposing term limits — which, after 24 years in Congress, would be an awkward conversation anyway. She helped negotiate the Bipartisan Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act in 2021, which is genuinely notable, but that lone bipartisan achievement sits in a sea of blocking popular reforms on drug pricing, paid family leave, and Pentagon accountability.

The Capito brand in West Virginia politics is generational. Her nephew, Riley Moore, now serves in the House representing WV-2, making the Moore-Capito family a two-chamber operation. When your grandfather went to prison for corruption, your father's name is on the building, and your nephew holds the neighboring seat, "public service" starts to look a lot like a family business — one where the voters of West Virginia keep paying the bills while the energy industry keeps signing the checks.

John Carter has represented Texas's 31st District since 2003, making him the third longest-serving member of the House at age 84. If term limits ever come up in conversation, Carter presumably excuses himself to the restroom and doesn't return. Twenty-three years in Congress and counting -- the man has held his seat longer than some of his constituents have been alive.

Carter chairs the Military Construction and Veterans Affairs Appropriations Subcommittee and sits on the Defense Subcommittee, a combination that has turned his campaign treasury into a defense contractor reunion. General Dynamics, BAE Systems, CACI International, and Honeywell International each wrote $5,000 checks. Lockheed Martin kicked in $3,500, Boeing added $1,500, and RTX Corporation (formerly Raytheon) and Blue Origin each contributed $1,000. In total, nearly $387,000 of his $1.1 million in receipts came from PACs. The companies writing checks are the same ones selling hardware, software, and infrastructure to the military -- and Carter sits on the committee that decides how much to buy.

His district's northern half revolves around Fort Cavazos and its roughly 45,000 active-duty soldiers. Carter has secured tens of millions in appropriations for the base -- $29 million here, $48.7 million there -- which is wonderful for the troops and also wonderful for the defense contractors bankrolling his campaigns. In fiscal year 2010 alone, he sponsored or co-sponsored 27 earmarks totaling $42.2 million. Auditing the Pentagon? That might reveal uncomfortable questions about the pipeline between his donor list and his subcommittee docket.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Carter is a near-perfect zero. No support for banning congressional stock trading -- notable given that he amended his financial disclosure forms in 2009 to belatedly report nearly $300,000 in capital gains from ExxonMobil stock sales in 2006 and 2007. No interest in term limits despite being the living embodiment of why they exist. No movement on banning corporate PACs, dark money reform, the DISCLOSE Act, or any campaign finance legislation that might turn off the spigot. He endorsed Donald Trump, who endorsed him back in December 2025 as Carter faced a ten-way primary field -- his most crowded ever, suggesting even Republican voters in TX-31 are starting to wonder if 23 years is enough.

Carter's legislative philosophy can be summarized simply: protect the base, cash the checks, and never, ever vote for anything that might make the defense lobby reconsider its generosity.

Tom Cole has been in Congress since 2003 — long enough to qualify for a geological survey — and in that time has ascended to Chairman of the House Appropriations Committee, the body that decides how $1.7 trillion in annual federal spending gets carved up. He is the first Native American and first Oklahoman to hold the gavel, which he wields with the quiet authority of a man who has been cashing defense industry checks for two decades and sees no reason to stop now [1].

Cole is, by his own admission, a man who will never support cutting defense spending [2]. This is convenient, given that defense contractors and oil and gas interests have been among his most reliable donor industries throughout his career [3]. He chairs the committee that writes the Pentagon's checks, and the Pentagon has failed every single one of its independent audits since Congress mandated them in 2018. Cole's response to this staggering record of financial incompetence has been to keep signing bigger checks. Auditing the Pentagon is apparently only a priority when the Pentagon is not one of your top funders.

On stock trading bans, Cole has been conspicuously silent. No co-sponsorships, no floor speeches, no public enthusiasm for the idea that the man controlling $1.7 trillion in spending decisions should perhaps not also be trading individual stocks. On term limits, Cole — who has served eleven terms — offers a living rebuttal to the concept. He voted against the For the People Act, which included provisions for campaign finance transparency and election reform [4]. The DISCLOSE Act, which would expose dark money in elections, has never found a friend in his office.

Cole does deserve credit for his work on Native American issues. As Republican Co-Chair of the Congressional Native American Caucus, he has consistently fought for tribal funding and was instrumental in directing resources to Indian Country even during budget cuts [5]. It is a genuine, substantive legacy — and it makes his indifference to virtually every other populist reform on the 80% Bill all the more pointed.

He has taken corporate PAC money throughout his career, opposed paid family leave legislation, and shown no interest in banning junk fees, closing tax loopholes for the ultra-wealthy, or supporting the right to repair. On the 80% Bill scorecard, Cole is a case study in how institutional power works in Washington: you rise by serving the interests that fund your rise, and by the time you hold the gavel, the question of whom you serve has already been answered.

Ed Case is the kind of Democrat who makes other Democrats nervous. A fourth-generation Hawaian from Hilo, Case practiced corporate law at Carlsmith Ball for nearly two decades before entering politics, rising to managing partner before pivoting to the Hawaii House of Representatives in 1994 [1]. He led a coup against his own party's leadership in the state legislature, got elected Majority Leader, and has been charming his way across the aisle ever since [2]. Now representing Hawaii's 1st Congressional District -- urban Honolulu -- he sits on the powerful House Appropriations Committee, the first Hawaiian on that panel in three decades, with a perch on the Defense Subcommittee that just happens to oversee billions in military spending for a state where the Pentagon is the largest employer [3].

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Case is a study in selective enthusiasm. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, calling it a "game changer" for Hawaii [4]. He cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act to curb dark money and backed the For the People Act's campaign finance reforms [5]. But when it comes to banning congressional stock trading, imposing term limits, or auditing the Pentagon -- the body that funnels roughly $1.3 billion in military construction to his state annually -- Case's enthusiasm dims to a polite silence [6]. His record on ending forever wars is similarly ambiguous for a man whose committee literally writes the defense budget.

Then there is the matter of who pays for Ed Case's campaigns. In the first quarter of his 2019 term, a staggering 99.4 percent of his contributions came from political action committees -- not a typo, ninety-nine point four percent [7]. His donor rolls read like a corporate hospitality directory: American Crystal Sugar, the American Hotel & Lodging Association, Marriott International, Hilton Worldwide, plus a healthy sprinkling of defense contractors including General Dynamics, Honeywell, and Raytheon [8]. Case has defended this arrangement with the logic that PAC money is fine "as long as he knows where it's coming from" -- a standard so low it could limbo under a parking curb [9]. For a congressman who theoretically supports the DISCLOSE Act's transparency goals, the irony of being the most PAC-dependent member of the Hawaii delegation is apparently lost on him.

Case's willingness to cross party lines has only grown more conspicuous. In April 2025, he was one of just four Democrats to vote for the Republican-backed SAVE Act, requiring documentary proof of citizenship to vote -- a bill his own state attorney general was suing to block [10]. He is a proud member of the Blue Dog Coalition and the Problem Solvers Caucus, which in practice means he is a reliable vote for bipartisan defense spending and an unreliable vote for anything that might upset the corporate PACs keeping his campaign flush [11]. Hawaii's voters deserve to ask: when 99 percent of your money comes from PACs and defense contractors, whose interests are you solving problems for?

If Washington had a concierge service for corporate lobbyists, John Cornyn would be the five-star maître d'. The senior senator from Texas has spent over two decades perfecting the art of taking money from every major industry in America while delivering exactly what they paid for. Lobbyists representing Goldman Sachs, Koch Industries, and Lockheed Martin bundled nearly $1.1 million for his coffers in a single cycle — more lobbyist-bundler cash than any other politician raised at the time. As one Texas watchdog put it: "This guy has got a long history of taking money from big business and delivering for big business."

On the DISCLOSE Act, which would require dark money groups to reveal donors contributing more than $10,000, Cornyn has been a reliable vote against transparency. He framed the bill as a threat to "ordinary Americans exercising First Amendment rights" — a touching defense of the constitutional freedoms of anonymous billionaires. Senate Republicans blocked the bill in a party-line vote in 2022, with Cornyn dutifully holding the line. He even introduced his own bill to address "foreign adversary" influence, apparently unbothered by the domestic variety that keeps his campaign flush.

Then there are the Epstein files. When the Senate voted on compelling Attorney General Pam Bondi to release the documents, Cornyn voted to table the measure. His stated reason? The files were "a great distraction from the issues that make a meaningful difference in the lives of ordinary Americans." Nothing says "ordinary Americans" quite like shielding the client list of a convicted sex trafficker.

Cornyn voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, killing his chance to support Medicare drug price negotiation — even though he later introduced a separate bipartisan bill to lower prescription drug costs for seniors. The cynic might note that his version conveniently lacked the teeth of the IRA's negotiation program. He called Medicare drug price negotiation "price fixing" that "inevitably leads to scarcity," parroting pharmaceutical industry talking points with the fluency of a man who has received significant pharma contributions over a 20-plus year Senate career.

On guns, Cornyn earned a rare moment of bipartisan credit by shepherding the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act through the Senate in 2022 — the largest federal gun safety package in three decades. For this act of minimal decency after the Uvalde massacre in his own state, he was booed at the Texas Republican Convention. He has collected roughly $340,000 in career contributions from pro-gun donors, making that one vote a rather expensive act of conscience.

Twenty-three years in the Senate, a lobbying apparatus that would make K Street blush, and a voting record that reads like a corporate wish list. John Cornyn is not working for Texas — he is working for whoever is writing the biggest check.

Jim Costa is a third-generation Central Valley farmer who has represented the San Joaquin Valley in Congress since 2005, making him one of the longest-serving House Democrats in California. His district, CA-21, sits at the heart of one of the most productive agricultural regions on Earth, generating over $24 billion in crops annually [1]. A former chair of the Blue Dog Coalition, Costa has built a career on the brand of fiscal moderation that, depending on the vote, can look like either principled centrism or convenient flexibility [2]. He serves as a senior member of the House Agriculture Committee, where he has helped craft three Farm Bills, and on the House Foreign Affairs Committee [3].

On the substance of the 80% Bill, Costa's record is a patchwork of genuine alignment and conspicuous silence. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation and climate provisions, and was the only member from the central San Joaquin Valley to do so [4]. He voted for the Build Back Better Act, which contained paid family leave provisions [5]. He co-sponsored and voted for the For the People Act (H.R. 1), which addressed gerrymandering, dark money disclosure, and campaign finance reform in one sweeping package [6]. He voted for the John R. Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act [7]. And when the Epstein Files Transparency Act came to the House floor in November 2025, he was among the 427 members who voted yes, a vote that required roughly the same political courage as endorsing sunshine [8].

But the Blue Dog instincts that make Costa palatable to his purple-ish district also produce moments that make progressives wince. In March 2025, he was one of just ten Democrats to vote with every House Republican to censure Rep. Al Green for disrupting Trump's State of the Union address, a move critics called "cowardly" and "spineless" [9]. Costa's statement tried to thread the needle, saying he agreed with Green's claims about protecting Medicare and Social Security but that he "respectfully disagreed with disruptions during official proceedings" [10]. He has missed 5.4% of all roll call votes since 2005, nearly triple the median for current representatives, though some absences were due to a medical procedure in 2017 [11]. On issues like banning congressional stock trading, lifetime lobbying bans, and congressional term limits, Costa's public position is best described as a shrug emoji — notable for a man who has spent over two decades in the same House seat and whose estimated net worth of $5.4 million includes significant holdings in crop production and real estate [12].

Follow the money and the picture sharpens. Costa's personal financial disclosures show approximately $3 million in crop production and basic processing assets and over $1.1 million in real estate [13]. He sits on the Agriculture Committee that directly regulates the industry in which he is personally invested, and over 75 agricultural organizations endorsed his bid to become Ranking Member of that committee [14]. His campaign has historically drawn from agribusiness, real estate, and energy interests — the very sectors whose regulatory frameworks he shapes [15]. Costa is not a villain in this story; he is something more instructive — a long-tenured Democrat whose voting record on popular reforms is just good enough to avoid scrutiny, and just spotty enough to suggest that the 80% Bill's full menu would give him heartburn.

Emanuel Cleaver II is a Democrat representing Missouri's 5th Congressional District, which covers Kansas City and its suburbs. Before Congress, Cleaver was a Methodist pastor at St. James United Methodist Church for nearly four decades and served as Kansas City's first African American mayor from 1991 to 1999[1][2]. Now in his eleventh term, he sits on the House Financial Services Committee, where he serves as Ranking Member of the Subcommittee on Housing and Insurance[3]. A preacher-turned-politician who has been in Washington since 2005, Cleaver has had two decades to practice what he preaches. The congregation should check the receipts.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Cleaver stacks up well on paper. He co-introduced the bipartisan TRUST in Congress Act to ban members of Congress, their spouses, and dependent children from trading individual stocks, declaring that "we don't allow athletes to bet on games in which they can have a direct impact" and the same standard should apply to lawmakers[4][5][6]. He introduced the Camera Authorization and Maintenance Act of 2014, requiring law enforcement agencies receiving DOJ grants to equip officers with body cameras, and helped secure $22.5 million in federal funding for the program[7]. He voted to release the Epstein files, stating he was "proud to vote for the bill" in hopes of providing closure to survivors[8][9]. He co-sponsored the For the People Act (H.R. 1), the sweeping voting rights and anti-gerrymandering legislation[10]. He applauded the Inflation Reduction Act's Medicare drug price negotiation provisions[11], and has vowed to "continue to fight to preserve" Social Security and Medicare[12].

Where Cleaver gets complicated is in the gap between his populist rhetoric and his financial reality. He co-sponsored the Justice in Policing Act alongside over 200 Democrats[13], and his positions on paid family leave and consumer protection generally track with the Democratic mainstream. But his voting attendance tells a different story of engagement: he has missed 6.3% of roll call votes over his career, far worse than the 2.0% median for sitting representatives[14].

Then there is the money. In the 2023-2024 cycle, a full 66.6% of Cleaver's funding came from PACs, with only 4.29% from small individual donors[15]. His top contributing industry was real estate at $277,913, followed by insurance at $188,540, securities and investment at $127,350, and commercial banking at $76,510[15]. For a man who sits on the Financial Services Committee overseeing the very industries writing him checks, the arrangement has a certain theological elegance: the banks give unto Cleaver, and Cleaver renders unto the banks the gift of oversight-shaped-like-a-handshake. It is worth noting that Cleaver has not co-sponsored the Ban Corporate PACs Act, which would sever exactly the pipeline that funds two-thirds of his campaign.

Cleaver is the kind of Democrat who will co-sign the right press release and show up for the right photo op, but whose donor sheet reads like a Financial Services Committee witness list. He is right on stock trading, right on body cameras, right on the Epstein files, and right on drug pricing in theory. But until the preacher starts practicing the full gospel of the 80% Bill, particularly banning the corporate PAC money that keeps his lights on, the sermon rings a little hollow.
